framework: A path qualifies only when >=3 prominent, proven people in the field visibly share the capability, each with a resolving link — the pattern across standouts is the evidence, not market demand. Ranked by strength of that pattern. Curated editorial, not DOL-derived.

paths.what_it_is: Turning your own operating knowledge into an open, ongoing public resource — a membership community, podcast, or newsletter — where other operations professionals learn and swap frameworks.
paths.why_it_signals: When several standout operators independently choose to build audience-scale communities rather than stay heads-down inside one company, it shows they can abstract tacit ops know-how into frameworks other people can actually use, which is the differentiator senior ops hiring managers look for.

paths.path: Codifying an operating playbook into a book or named system
paths.what_it_is: Writing down the operating cadence, meeting rhythm, and decision system you run internally as a structured, named methodology (a book, a framework, a certification) that other companies can pick up and run themselves.
paths.why_it_signals: Multiple prominent operators converging on the same move — packaging their internal playbook as a transferable system — shows a rarer skill than execution: designing operations as reusable architecture instead of one-off firefighting, which is exactly what separates a senior operator from a task-taker.

paths.path: Defining the chief-of-staff craft in public
paths.what_it_is: Building a structured, ongoing public teaching practice — a certification course, coaching community, or long-running publication — that turns the ambiguous chief-of-staff / strategic-operator role into a learnable, describable craft.
paths.why_it_signals: Three independent operators building sustained public teaching practices around the same ambiguous role shows a repeatable skill: naming and structuring an undefined job well enough that other people can be trained into it, a strong proxy for the judgment strategy-and-ops hiring managers actually want.
